name: Cher
description:
'''Cher''' ({{IPAc-en|ʃ|ɛər|audio=LL-Q1860 (eng)-Naomi Persephone Amethyst (NaomiAmethyst)-Cher.wav}} {{respell|SHAIR}}; legally [[Mononym|mononymous]];{{efn|She [[Name change|legally adopted]] the [[mononym]] Cher in 1979, dropping her first name Cheryl along with the surnames Sarkisian (paternal), LaPiere (her stepfather's) and the married names Bono and Allman.<ref name=mononym/>}} born '''Cheryl Sarkisian''',{{efn|name=Cheryl|Cher's birth certificate lists her name as Cheryl Sarkisian, contrary to the long-reported Cherilyn. Cher later discovered this when formally changing her name to "Cher", attributing the discrepancy to her mother's recollection of naming her after actress [[Lana Turner]]'s daughter, [[Cheryl Crane]], and her grandmother, Lynda.<ref>{{cite web |last=Harvilla |first=Rob |date=November 19, 2024 |title=14 Stunning Stories From Cher's (First) Memoir |url=https://www.vulture.com/article/cher-the-memoir-best-stories-part-one.html |website=[[Vulture (website)|Vulture]] |access-date=November 22, 2024 |quote="Cher writes that when she later formally changed her name, she found out that the name on her birth certificate was, to her own mom's surprise, not Cherilyn but Cheryl. 'I was only a teenager and I was in a lot of pain,' Cher's mother told her. 'Give me a break.'" |archive-date=November 22, 2024 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20241122212601/https://www.vulture.com/article/cher-the-memoir-best-stories-part-one.html |url-status=live}}</ref>}} May 20, 1946) is an American singer and actress. Dubbed the "'''Goddess of Pop'''", she is known for her [[Androgyny|androgynous]], [[contralto]] voice, [[Cultural impact of Cher#Fashion|bold fashion]], [[Cultural impact of Cher#Stage and television|elaborate stagecraft]] and multifaceted career. [[Cher filmography|Her screen roles]] often reflect [[Cultural impact of Cher#Provocation and controversy|her public image]] as a strong-willed, outspoken woman. An [[Cultural impact of Cher|influential figure in popular culture]], Cher has sustained a career spanning more than six decades through continual [[Cultural impact of Cher#Reinvention|reinvention]].

Cher rose to fame in 1965 as part of the [[folk rock]] duo [[Sonny & Cher]], whose hit single "[[I Got You Babe]]" became emblematic of [[1960s counterculture]]. She simultaneously launched a solo career with moody pop songs such as "[[Bang Bang (My Baby Shot Me Down)]]", whose theatrical [[storytelling]] foreshadowed her 1970s [[Billboard Hot 100|US ''Billboard'' Hot 100]] number-one singles "[[Gypsys, Tramps & Thieves]]", "[[Half-Breed (song)|Half-Breed]]" and "[[Dark Lady (song)|Dark Lady]]". After a period of acting, she released the [[hair metal]] albums ''[[Cher (1987 album)|Cher]]'' (1987), ''[[Heart of Stone (Cher album)|Heart of Stone]]'' (1989) and ''[[Love Hurts (Cher album)|Love Hurts]]'' (1991), scoring international top-ten hits "[[If I Could Turn Back Time]]" and "[[The Shoop Shoop Song (It's in His Kiss)#Cher version|The Shoop Shoop Song (It's in His Kiss)]]". At 52, she released the [[dance-pop]] album ''[[Believe (Cher album)|Believe]]'' (1998), which introduced the "Cher effect"—a stylized use of [[Auto-Tune]] to distort vocals. Its [[Believe (Cher song)|title track]] became [[Billboard Year-End Hot 100 singles of 1999|1999's number-one song in the US]] and [[List of best-selling singles in the United Kingdom|the UK's best-selling single by a female artist]]. In the 21st century, she released her highest-charting [[Billboard 200|US ''Billboard'' 200]] albums, ''[[Closer to the Truth]]'' (2013) and ''[[Dancing Queen (album)|Dancing Queen]]'' (2018), each debuting at number three.

Cher became a TV star in the 1970s with her [[CBS]] variety shows ''[[The Sonny & Cher Comedy Hour]]'', which drew more than 30 million weekly viewers, and the solo ''[[Cher (TV series)|Cher]]'', on which she became the first woman to deliberately expose her navel on American television after fighting [[Broadcast Standards and Practices|network censors]]. She later gained critical acclaim with her [[Broadway theatre|Broadway]] debut and the [[Come Back to the 5 & Dime, Jimmy Dean, Jimmy Dean (film)|film adaptation]] of ''[[Come Back to the 5 & Dime, Jimmy Dean, Jimmy Dean (play)|Come Back to the 5 & Dime, Jimmy Dean, Jimmy Dean]]'' (1982). Transitioning to film, she earned two [[Academy Award]] nominations—for ''[[Silkwood]]'' (1983) and ''[[Moonstruck]]'' (1987), winning [[Academy Award for Best Actress|Best Actress]] for the latter—and received the [[Cannes Film Festival's Best Actress Award]] for ''[[Mask (1985 film)|Mask]]'' (1985). Other starring roles include ''[[The Witches of Eastwick (film)|The Witches of Eastwick]]'' (1987), ''[[Mermaids (1990 film)|Mermaids]]'' (1990), ''[[If These Walls Could Talk]]'' (1996, her directorial debut), ''[[Tea with Mussolini]]'' (1999), ''[[Burlesque (2010 American film)|Burlesque]]'' (2010) and ''[[Mamma Mia! Here We Go Again]]'' (2018).

One of [[List of best-selling music artists|the best-selling music artists in history]], Cher has sold over 100 million records and is the only solo artist with number-one singles on the [[Billboard charts|US ''Billboard'' charts]] in [[Cultural impact of Cher#In music|seven consecutive decades (1960s–2020s)]]. [[List of awards and nominations received by Cher|Her accolades]] include two [[Grammy Award]]s—among them the [[Grammy Lifetime Achievement Award]]—a [[Primetime Emmy Award]], three [[Golden Globe Awards]] and the [[Kennedy Center Honors]]. She is the only performer to have won an Academy Award for acting and been [[List of Rock and Roll Hall of Fame inductees|inducted]] into the [[Rock and Roll Hall of Fame]]. Her 2002–2005 [[Living Proof: The Farewell Tour]] was [[List of highest-grossing concert tours|the highest-grossing concert tour by a female artist]] at the time, earning $250 million—about ${{inflation|US|250|2005|r=-1|fmt=c}} million in {{inflation/year|US}}. Her life and career inspired the 2018 [[jukebox musical]] ''[[The Cher Show (musical)|The Cher Show]]''. Beyond entertainment, Cher is known for her [[Progressivism in the United States|progressive]] politics, [[Cher as a gay icon|status as a gay icon]] and advocacy for causes including [[LGBTQ rights by country or territory|LGBTQ rights]] and [[HIV/AIDS]] awareness.
